symbols: woman grasping Joseph's garment — the allure of sin and the need to flee temptation
Joseph fleeing — righteous resistance to sexual temptation at great personal cost
red curtains of the bed chamber — passion, danger, and the trap of sin
discarded garment — the false evidence that will be used against the innocent
description: A dramatic bedroom scene showing a nude woman reclining on a luxurious bed with red curtains and ornate coverings. She reaches toward a young man in dark clothing who turns away, his garment caught in her grasp as he flees toward the right edge of the composition. The woman's body is brightly lit against the dark interior, while Joseph is shown in motion, twisting away from her reach. The setting is richly appointed with patterned textiles and sumptuous drapery.
theological_description: The story of Joseph and Potiphar's wife (GEN.39.6-20) is one of Scripture's great moral dramas. Joseph, sold into slavery by his brothers, rises to oversee Potiphar's household, but his master's wife 'cast her eyes on Joseph and said, "Lie with me"' (GEN.39.7). Day after day Joseph refuses, declaring, 'How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?' (GEN.39.9). When she seizes his garment, he flees, leaving it in her hand — and she uses it as false evidence to have him imprisoned. Joseph's flight embodies the New Testament counsel to 'flee from sexual immorality' (1CO.6.18). His suffering for righteousness — false accusation and imprisonment — prefigures Christ's own unjust condemnation. The garment left behind becomes an instrument of injustice, yet God uses even this betrayal to advance his plan, as Joseph's imprisonment leads to his eventual elevation to Pharaoh's right hand.
